ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for highball cocktail. Closest parent 'alcoholic beverage food product' (FOODON_03301589) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code F00DP (Spirits and spirit-based drinks) is the closest parent category for a whisky-and-soda highball. No exact FoodEx2 term for highball exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 53041004 (Alcoholic beverage) is the closest parent. No specific SNOMED CT code for highball cocktail.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard Japanese-style highball is typically served in a tall glass containing approximately 45 ml of whisky topped with carbonated water over ice, totalling around 300 ml.

nutrient_source.title: Alcoholic beverage, distilled, whisky — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14037); Carbonated water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14412); combined estimate for highball (1:5 whisky-to-soda ratio)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: A highball is approximately 1 part whisky (40% ABV) to 4–5 parts carbonated water. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by blending USDA SR Legacy values for whisky (NDB 14037) and carbonated water (NDB 14412) at a 1:5 ratio by volume (approximately 17 g whisky : 83 g carbonated water per 100 g serving). Alcohol content drives energy; protein, fat, and carbohydrate are effectively zero from both components.

gi_source.title: Alcoholic beverages and glycemic response — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature; highball contains no glycemic carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: A highball (whisky and carbonated water) contains no glycemic carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign a GI to pure spirits or spirit-and-soda mixers because they contain no digestible carbohydrate. glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) per Project Veritas convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages; gi_category is low; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero.

hypertension.key_factor: Alcohol content and acute vasodilatory-then-pressor effect
hypertension.guidance: Regular alcohol consumption raises blood pressure over time; individuals with hypertension should limit highball intake to no more than one standard drink per occasion and monitor blood pressure response, as even moderate alcohol use is associated with a 1–2 mmHg rise in systolic pressure per drink per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Alcohol-induced hypoglycaemia risk and caloric contribution from ethanol
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although a highball contains no glycemic carbohydrates and will not directly raise blood glucose, the ethanol in whisky can inhibit hepatic gluconeogenesis and cause delayed hypoglycaemia, particularly in individuals taking insulin or sulfonylureas; people with Type 2 Diabetes should consume highballs only with food and in moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While low-to-moderate alcohol intake has been associated with modest HDL elevation in some epidemiological studies, regular consumption of spirits including highballs is linked to increased risk of atrial fibrillation and cardiomyopathy; individuals with established cardiovascular disease should restrict alcohol to within national guidelines (no more than 1–2 standard drinks per day) or abstain entirely on medical advice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A highball (ハイボール, Haibōru) is a mixed alcoholic beverage consisting of a spirit — most commonly Japanese whisky — combined with a larger proportion of carbonated water, served over ice in a tall glass. In Japan, the highball became enormously popular from the 2000s onward, driven by Suntory's marketing of its Kakubin whisky as a refreshing, low-calorie alternative to beer. The standard Japanese preparation uses a ratio of approximately 1 part whisky to 4–5 parts chilled sparkling water, yielding a light, effervescent drink with a clean whisky aroma and a crisp, slightly dry finish. The drink contains virtually no protein, fat, or carbohydrate; its caloric content derives entirely from ethanol, contributing approximately 40 kcal per 100 g at a typical 1:5 dilution. Sodium is minimal (approximately 5 mg per 100 g from the carbonated water). Because it contains no glycemic carbohydrates, it does not raise blood glucose directly, but ethanol can suppress hepatic glucose output and pose a hypoglycaemia risk for people with diabetes on insulin or secretagogues. In Singapore, highballs are served at Japanese izakayas and ramen restaurants such as Ramen Matsuri as an accompaniment to savoury dishes. The drink is not halal due to its alcohol content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a highball?
What whisky is used in a Japanese highball?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB, ICMR-NIN, USDA) provides a direct entry for a Japanese-style highball cocktail. Nutrient values are estimated by blending USDA SR Legacy data for whisky and carbonated water at a standard 1:5 ratio. The estimation methodology is transparent and defensible, but the result is an approximation rather than a laboratory-measured value, warranting medium confidence.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero: whisky and carbonated water contain no protein by composition; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for distilled spirits (NDB 14037) and carbonated water (NDB 14412). 2. fat_g is naturally zero: neither whisky nor carbonated water contains fat; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero: standard Japanese highball uses plain carbonated water with no added sugar; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. sugar_g is naturally zero by product composition. 5. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a liquid alcoholic beverage with no plant fibre. 6. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per Project Veritas convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages. 7. iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, cholesterol_mg are all 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for distilled spirits and carbonated water). 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are 0.0 confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. 9. The input food_group was listed as 'Grains and staples' which is incorrect for an alcoholic beverage; corrected to 'Beverages'. 10. The cuisine_tags 'Ramen' and 'Noodle' in the input reflect the restaurant context (Ramen Matsuri) rather than the food itself; the highball is a Japanese cocktail, not a noodle dish. 11. Serving size of 300 g is based on a standard Japanese highball glass (approximately 300 ml, density approximately 1.0 g/ml). 12. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301589 and FoodEx2 code F00DP are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology codes exist for highball cocktail in any of the three ontology systems reviewed.
